content.text:
<recommended_plugins>
Here is a list of plugins that are available but not installed. If the user's query would benefit from one of these plugins, use the `request_plugin_install` tool to suggest that they install it. Pass the parenthesized ID as `plugin_id`. For example, suggest the Google Drive plugin if the query could possibly be better answered with access to Google Drive.

- Atlassian Rovo (atlassian-rovo@openai-curated-remote)
- Box (box@openai-curated-remote)
- Gmail (gmail@openai-curated-remote)
- Google Calendar (google-calendar@openai-curated-remote)
- Outlook Calendar (outlook-calendar@openai-curated-remote)
- Outlook Email (outlook-email@openai-curated-remote)
- SharePoint (sharepoint@openai-curated-remote)
- Teams (teams@openai-curated-remote)
</recommended_plugins>
i want you to go through the codebase with 10 different code-reaper subagents and tell me what we should clean up in this codebase to make it more maintainable.

content.text:
Persistence/database audit complete. No edits made. No P0 findings.

1. P1 — synchronous persistence facade blocks async request threads and leaks a Tokio runtime

   - Evidence: [`runtime.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/runtime.rs:3) creates a private Tokio runtime per store. `run_on_runtime` spawns work, then blocks on `std::sync::mpsc::Receiver::recv()` at lines 52–66. `Drop` intentionally `mem::forget`s the runtime at lines 21–27.
   - Scope: 61 `run_api_db_on(...)` call sites wrap otherwise async SeaORM operations; `requests.rs` lines 41–48 are a representative simple read.
   - Impact: every DB call has cross-runtime/mpsc ceremony, async Axum handlers block their executor thread, shutdown cannot be clean, and every persistence signature/call site is more complex.
   - Cleanup: make `MetadataStore` methods `async`, store only `DatabaseConnection`, await SeaORM directly, and delete `DbRuntime`, `run_api_db_on`, and `run_db_on`.

2. P1 — memory and Postgres are two hand-maintained persistence implementations, and they already disagree

   - Evidence: [`mod.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/mod.rs:78) defines the backend enum; there are 65 `MetadataStoreInner::Memory` branches across about 7,570 lines of DB modules. [`requests.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/requests.rs:77) sorts memory results by `created_at_unix` then ID, while [`request_rows.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/request_rows.rs:110) orders Postgres by ID only.
   - The memory backend is not merely tests: local dev installs it in [`dev/api/mod.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/dev/api/mod.rs:38) and [`dev/api/seed.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/dev/api/seed.rs:772).
   - Impact: local/dev behavior and many tests can pass against semantics production does not have. Every new command duplicates transaction, lookup, mutation, and persistence behavior.
   - Cleanup: given pre-alpha constraints, delete `memory-metadata`; run local dev and integration tests against disposable Postgres. Keep pure domain tests against `AppCatalog` directly, without pretending it is a second database.

3. P1 — one global lock row serializes unrelated metadata work

   - Evidence: [`locks.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/locks.rs:8) creates the single `"catalog"` lock row; lines 28–54 take shared/update locks on it. There are 39 write-lock call sites. For example, granting one user credits at [`requests.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/requests.rs:162), starting any request at line 206, and mutating any repository at [`repo_mutation.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/repo_mutation.rs:37) all contend on the same row.
   - Impact: independent users/repos cannot mutate concurrently, and persistence code relies on a process-wide catalog model rather than database constraints and aggregate ownership.
   - Cleanup: after removing the memory parity requirement, lock only the affected repository/request/account rows with `FOR UPDATE`; use unique constraints/upserts for idempotency. Retain explicit multi-row transactions where domain invariants actually span rows.

4. P1 — `MetadataStore::read` hydrates the entire database for narrow production reads

   - Evidence: [`mod.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/mod.rs:136) routes Postgres `read` through `load_catalog`. [`load_catalog_async`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/mod.rs:444) loads every user, repository, member, invite, cleanup job, request, request event, credit account, and ledger entry.
   - Production examples:
     - admin cleanup status needs only two queues: [`admin.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/http/admin.rs:130)
     - collaboration listing needs one repo and related users: [`repo_collaboration.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/http/repo_collaboration.rs:25)
     - resolving one member’s user loads everything: [`repo_collaboration.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/http/repo_collaboration.rs:281)
     - checking blob references hydrates the catalog: [`state.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/state.rs:484)
   - Cleanup: remove public `read`/whole-catalog loading from production. Add focused queue, collaboration, user, and blob-reference queries. Keep `load_catalog_async` only in test seeding/assertion support if still useful.

5. P2 — every repository save rewrites the full aggregate and schedules a projection rebuild

   - Evidence: [`save_repository_row`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/repository_rows.rs:49) updates every repository column, deletes/reinserts all settings/tokens/snapshot rows at lines 103–165, deletes/reinserts every member and invite at lines 168–198, then always enqueues a projection rebuild at line 99.
   - [`save_repo_mutation`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/repo_effects.rs:22) funnels all repository mutations through this. Thus creating an invite at [`repo_collaboration.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/repo_collaboration.rs:32) or changing settings at [`repo_settings.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/repo_settings.rs:14) rewrites unrelated rows and schedules projection work.
   - Cleanup: persist mutation-owned deltas: collaboration commands write invite/member rows; settings update settings/core columns; graph/policy changes alone enqueue projection rebuilds. This gives side effects clear ownership and eliminates broad delete/reinsert behavior.

6. P2 — schema is manually declared twice, while drift protection contains dead machinery

   - Evidence: 24 tables are duplicated between SeaORM models under [`entities/`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/entities/mod.rs:1) and 928 lines of custom definitions under [`schema_contract/`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/schema_contract/mod.rs:1). Repository columns alone appear in [`entities/repositories.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/entities/repositories.rs:3) and again in [`schema_contract/repositories.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/schema_contract/repositories.rs:3).
   - [`migrate_metadata_schema`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/schema.rs:13) appears to preserve populated databases unless drift is “destructive,” but `metadata_schema_drift` at lines 228–252 can return only `missing table`, `missing column`, or `column mismatch`; `is_destructive_pre_alpha_reset_drift` at lines 304–308 declares all three destructive. Therefore the row-scanning condition and error branch at lines 16–25 are unreachable for every reported drift.
   - Drift checks also compare names/counts only, not types, indexes, or foreign keys, despite the large contract.
   - Cleanup: choose one source of truth. For pre-alpha, the simplest option is conventional schema initialization/migrations plus generated SeaORM entities and an explicit destructive reset command. Delete `counts_for_catalog_rows`, reset ordering/row scans, and the pseudo-preservation branch.

7. P3 — persistence modules are organized by broad feature, then repeat transaction plumbing until they approach the project’s file limit

   - [`requests.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/requests.rs:1) is 973 lines and contains reads plus roughly 15 commands; [`repo_reads.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/repo_reads.rs:1) is 909 lines; [`cleanup_queue.rs`](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/cleanup_queue.rs:1) is 705 lines.
   - Cleanup: first remove backend/runtime duplication; then split only along behavior-owned boundaries such as request queries, lifecycle commands, settlement/credits, and cleanup-job claiming/reconciliation. Avoid adding another generic repository abstraction.

Sound patterns worth preserving:

- Domain transitions already live outside DB code and return explicit mutation/effect values.
- Row/domain conversion is mostly centralized in entity modules.
- Focused read models in `repo_reads.rs` and transaction-local helpers in `request_rows.rs` are the right direction.
- Cleanup and outbox work use durable tables, idempotency keys/generations, and explicit transactions rather than fire-and-forget side effects.
